title: Quiz: One-Time Signatures
description: Test your understanding of Lamport and Winternitz signature schemes

questions.question: In a Lamport signature, what determines which secret value is revealed for each bit position?

options.text: A random choice made during signing
The corresponding bit of the message hash
The position index modulo 2
The public key value at that position

questions.explanation: For each bit position i, if bit i of H(message) is 0, reveal sk_0[i]; if it's 1, reveal sk_1[i]. This links the signature to the specific message being signed.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of WOTS over Lamport signatures?

options.text: WOTS can sign multiple messages safely
WOTS has significantly smaller signatures
WOTS is faster to verify
WOTS uses less random data

questions.explanation: WOTS uses hash chains instead of pairs, encoding the message in base-w instead of base-2. With w=16, this reduces the number of signature elements by ~4× compared to Lamport.

questions.question: In WOTS, what is the purpose of the checksum?

options.text: To detect transmission errors
To compress the signature
To prevent forgeries where message digits are increased
To verify the hash function is working correctly

questions.explanation: Without the checksum, an attacker could forge signatures by hashing signature values forward (increasing digits). The checksum decreases when any message digit increases, requiring the attacker to go backwards in a hash chain - which requires finding preimages.

questions.question: What is the time-space tradeoff controlled by the Winternitz parameter w?

options.text: Higher w means faster signing but larger signatures
Higher w means smaller signatures but more hash operations
Higher w means stronger security but slower verification
Higher w only affects key generation time

questions.explanation: Higher w means fewer chains (smaller signatures) but each chain has more steps (w-1 maximum hashes). With w=16, signature is ~4× smaller than w=4, but requires up to 15 hashes per chain instead of 3.

questions.question: For 256-bit security with Lamport signatures, approximately how large is the public key?

options.text: 256 bytes
1 KB
8 KB
16 KB

questions.explanation: Lamport needs 2n secret values of n bits each. For n=256: PK = 2 × 256 × 32 bytes = 16,384 bytes = 16 KB. This massive size is why WOTS and other optimizations are important.

questions.question: In WOTS verification, how does the verifier check the signature is valid?

options.text: Decrypt the signature using the public key
Hash each signature element once and compare to public key
Complete each hash chain from signature position to the end and compare to public key
Run the same signing algorithm and compare outputs

questions.explanation: For each chain i, the verifier hashes signature[i] for (w-1 - digit[i]) more steps to reach the chain endpoint, then checks if it matches pk[i]. This completes the chain without knowing the starting secret.

questions.question: What additional feature does WOTS+ add compared to basic WOTS?

options.text: Ability to sign multiple messages
Domain separation using addresses and bitmasks
Shorter public keys
Quantum resistance

questions.explanation: WOTS+ adds domain separation through address-based hash function instances and XOR bitmasks before hashing. This enables cleaner security proofs and prevents multi-target attacks when WOTS+ is used as a building block in larger schemes.

questions.question: For WOTS with n=256 bits and w=16, how many hash chains are needed (l = l1 + l2)?
questions.explanation: With w=16 (log2(w)=4): l1 = ceil(256/4) = 64 chains for the message. l2 = floor(log2(64×15)/4) + 1 = 3 chains for checksum. Total l = 64 + 3 = 67 chains.

questions.question: The security of Lamport signatures relies on collision resistance of the hash function.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Lamport signatures only require preimage resistance. To forge, an attacker needs to find a preimage of a public key component, not a collision. This is actually a weaker (better) assumption than collision resistance.
